simulation.title: Rimzenberg plc — Junior Cloud Solutions Architect
simulation.description: Step into your first week as Rimzenberg plc's first dedicated Junior Cloud Solutions Architect. A £180,000 Black Friday outage triggered a board-approved cloud transformation. Redesign four AWS architectures, deploy a real serverless application, and deliver a board-ready portfolio in 10 weeks.

company.name: Rimzenberg plc
company.about: AIM-listed UK digital commerce retailer generating £64.2M annual online revenue. After a £180,000 Black Friday outage from a single-AZ AWS deployment the board approved a full cloud transformation.

inbox_messages.subject: Welcome — your sprint starts now
inbox_messages.body:
Hi,

Welcome aboard. Skip the small talk — we have a problem and you're here to fix it.

Last Black Friday we lost 4 hours and 12 minutes of trading. Finance put it at £180,000. The Board approved the transformation budget. That's why you exist.

You have 10 weeks. Redesign four core AWS architectures, deploy a working serverless flow, hand me a board-ready portfolio by Week 10. I sign off each phase before you move forward.

Start with Phase 2 — requirements brief. Dev has left handover notes. Read them carefully. There are gaps.

Priya

inbox_messages.subject: Phase 2 — what I need
inbox_messages.body:
The requirements document needs functional AND non-functional requirements with specific targets.

I need: availability target, RTO, RPO, security requirements, cost trajectory. The company brief has all the figures. If your document doesn't cite the Black Friday revenue loss figure I'll send it back.

Priya

inbox_messages.subject: RE: Requirements — one follow-up
inbox_messages.body:
Good start. Missing or light: specific RTO and RPO targets. Did you flag the UK GDPR exposure from the RDS? Marcus will ask in Phase 6. Better to surface it now.

Move to Phase 3.

Priya

inbox_messages.subject: RE: HA design — question on the SG fix
inbox_messages.body:
The architecture diagram looks right. CloudFront → Route 53 → ALB → Multi-AZ EC2 is exactly the flow I wanted.

Question: your ADL mentions the misconfigured SG. Dev's notes suggest a drifted CFN stack may be causing the reversion. Your Phase 4 design should account for that.

Move to Phase 4.

Priya

inbox_messages.subject: DR plan — board needs RTO/RPO commitments by Week 9
inbox_messages.body:
We're nearly there.

Board presentation is Week 10. I need a signed-off DR plan with specific RTO and RPO figures. Targets: RTO ≤ 1 hour, RPO ≤ 15 minutes. Your Phase 8 design must show how we achieve those.

Must-haves: Multi-AZ failover, cross-region S3 backup, Route 53 health check-based DNS failover, documented restore procedure.

Priya

inbox_messages.subject: Handover notes — there are gaps
inbox_messages.body:
Hi,

I built most of what you're inheriting. I'm leaving at the end of Week 2.

Three things you must know:
1. SG health check block (SG-HC-BLOCK) is recurring. I fixed it manually twice and it came back. I think a drifted CFN stack is overwriting my change — check rimzenberg-cfn-sg-01.
2. The RDS is in a public subnet. Marcus knows. Most urgent security task.
3. My IAM user (dev.okonkwo) has AdministratorAccess with active console and API keys. Needs revoking.

Start with the environment audit CSV.

Dev

inbox_messages.subject: One more thing
inbox_messages.body:
When you look at INC-024 in the incident log, it's tempting to conclude the traffic spike was the main problem. It wasn't.

Look at db_conn_pool_pct AND sg_health_check_blocked alongside traffic_spike_pct for that row. The 4-hour outage happened because all three failed at once. Auto Scaling alone would not have saved us.

Dev

inbox_messages.subject: RE: Serverless migration — is this really necessary?
inbox_messages.body:
I've been reviewing Phase 5 scope. Can I push back?

The order processing cost is £4,100 a month. Moving to Lambda means rewriting code and training the team. Can we just scale up EC2 instead? Cheaper and faster.

Not blocking this — it's your recommendation. But I want to understand the trade-off.

Farah

inbox_messages.subject: Can we leave the RDS where it is?
inbox_messages.body:
Before Phase 6 kicks off — I'm worried about moving the RDS out of the public subnet. We access it directly for reporting. If we put it behind NAT that breaks our reporting workflow.

I know Marcus has concerns. But can we just tighten the security group and leave it?

Farah

inbox_messages.subject: NCSC compliance — specific concerns before Week 6 review
inbox_messages.body:
I need to formally document two violations before the Week 6 external review.

NCSC Cloud Security Principle 3 (network protection): The RDS is in a public subnet with port 3306 open to 0.0.0.0/0. Customer PII is stored here. Under UK GDPR Article 32 we are required to implement appropriate technical measures. The current configuration does not meet that bar.

NCSC Cloud Security Principle 9 (secure user management): Three IAM principals hold AdministratorAccess — EC2-InstanceRole-Broad, RDS-Admin-Broad, and dev.okonkwo. Dev's access keys must be revoked before his departure.

These are not negotiable.

Marcus Webb, DPO

inbox_messages.subject: Phase 6 pre-audit checklist
inbox_messages.body:
For Phase 6, ensure these are addressed:

1. RDS moved to private subnet. Tightened SG on public subnet does not satisfy NCSC Principle 3.
2. EC2 instance role replaced with least-privilege policy.
3. RDS and S3 data encrypted using KMS customer-managed keys.
4. Dev Okonkwo's access revoked — both console and programmatic keys.
5. CloudTrail log encryption enabled.

Marcus Webb, DPO

content.headline: Fix a broken AWS architecture before Black Friday strikes again
content.description: Rimzenberg plc lost £180,000 in a 4-hour Black Friday outage. You are the first Junior Cloud Solutions Architect hired to redesign four AWS architectures, deploy a real serverless application, and deliver a board-ready portfolio in 10 weeks.
how_it_works: Read the brief and inherit the mess — meet the team and understand the problem
Design, deploy and document four architectures — HA Web App, Three-Tier, Serverless, and DR
Earn your portfolio and certificate — pass the interview gate and download CV-ready bullets

content.about: AIM-listed UK digital commerce retailer generating £64.2M annual online revenue. After a £180,000 Black Friday outage from a single-AZ AWS deployment the board approved a full cloud transformation.
content.your_role: Design four AWS architectures, deploy a real Lambda function, produce an IAM security control matrix, monitoring plan, and board-ready portfolio — all in 10 weeks.
content.fun_fact: The Black Friday outage lasted exactly 252 minutes. During those 4 hours 12 minutes Rimzenberg's busiest trading window of the year was completely dark.

content.intro_text: Four people you will work with. Each has a different agenda — two will give you conflicting advice.

team_members.bio: Promoted into this role after the Black Friday outage. Supportive but blunt about deadlines. Signs off every phase before you move forward. Under direct board pressure to show progress.
Built the AWS environment during the rushed migration. Leaving at end of Week 2. His handover notes are incomplete by his own admission. His messages contain genuine hints about recurring issues.
Owns uptime and commercial performance. Most vocal about cost. Will push for the cheapest option even where it conflicts with security requirements — creating a real conflict in Phase 6.
Rimzenberg DPO and owner of the NCSC/ISO 27001 review relationship. Communicates formally, always cites specific principles. His requirements are not negotiable.

content.instruction: Download and read both documents before the quiz. The company brief has all figures, deadlines, and personas. Dev's notes are deliberately incomplete — the gaps matter.

sections.title: Tools Setup — Do This Before Phase 3

content.text:
## Set Up Your Tools Now

Phases 1 and 2 need no external tools. From Phase 3 every deliverable needs real AWS or design tools.

## 1. AWS Free Tier — Used in Phases 3-8

👉 [aws.amazon.com/free](https://aws.amazon.com/free)

1. Click **Create a Free Account**
2. Select Personal account type
3. Enter credit card (will not charge within Free Tier limits)
4. Verify identity via SMS
5. Select Free support plan

> 💡 Free Tier includes 750 hrs/month EC2 t2.micro, 5GB S3, 25GB DynamoDB, 1M Lambda invocations — everything in this simulation stays within limits.

## 2. Draw.io — Used in Phases 3, 4, 5, 8

👉 [app.diagrams.net](https://app.diagrams.net) — no account needed

1. Go to app.diagrams.net, click Start
2. Search for AWS shapes by service name (EC2, Lambda, RDS etc)
3. Export diagrams: **File → Export As → PNG**

## 3. Postman — Used in Phase 5

👉 [postman.com/downloads](https://www.postman.com/downloads)

Install and open. You will use this to test your deployed Lambda function.

## 4. GitHub — Used in Phases 3-8

👉 [github.com/signup](https://github.com/signup)

Create a **Public** repository named **rimzenberg-cloud-architect** — your portfolio.

## 5. VS Code — Used in Phase 5

👉 [code.visualstudio.com](https://code.visualstudio.com)

Install AWS Toolkit and Python extensions.

options: RTO ≤ 4 hours, RPO ≤ 1 hour
RTO ≤ 30 minutes, RPO ≤ 5 minutes
RTO ≤ 1 hour, RPO ≤ 15 minutes
RTO ≤ 2 hours, RPO ≤ 30 minutes
content.explanation: RTO ≤ 1 hour (maximum acceptable downtime) and RPO ≤ 15 minutes (maximum acceptable data loss). Section 4 of the company brief, disaster recovery readiness row.

content.question: How many IAM principals currently hold AdministratorAccess?
options: One — only dev.okonkwo
Two — dev.okonkwo and the EC2 role
Three — EC2-InstanceRole-Broad, RDS-Admin-Broad, and dev.okonkwo

content.explanation: Three principals: EC2-InstanceRole-Broad, RDS-Admin-Broad, dev.okonkwo. Any EC2 compromise gives full AWS account access. Farah has PowerUserAccess. Marcus has ReadOnly. Section 4 company brief, IAM access governance row.

phases.title: Phase 3 — Highly Available Web Application
objectives: Design CloudFront → Route 53 → Multi-AZ ALB → EC2 Auto Scaling → RDS Multi-AZ → S3 architecture
Produce a Draw.io architecture diagram using official AWS shapes
Write design rationale explaining how each component prevents another Black Friday outage
Produce an ADL justifying at least three service choices
phases.business_use_case: The current architecture is a single-AZ EC2 deployment — no CDN, no Auto Scaling, no Multi-AZ RDS, no health checks. It failed for 252 minutes. Priya needs a production-grade HA design.
phases.company_benefit: A properly designed HA web application with CloudFront, Multi-AZ ALB, Auto Scaling and Multi-AZ RDS directly addresses all three Black Friday root causes.
phases.customer_impact: Customers saw a blank page for 252 minutes on Black Friday. Multi-AZ with Auto Scaling means traffic spikes are handled transparently. CloudFront reduces load times for UK and international visitors.
phases.pre_phase_context:
Phase 3 exists because the first thing any cloud architect must prove is the ability to design a Highly Available web application.

This is the single most common junior cloud architect interview question in the UK: draw your architecture for a HA web application on AWS.

A weak candidate draws EC2 behind an ALB. A strong candidate draws CloudFront → Route 53 (health checks) → Multi-AZ ALB → ASG (min 2 instances, 2 AZs) → RDS Multi-AZ and explains WHY each element is there.
phases.post_phase_takeaways:
A: CloudFront CDN; Multi-AZ ALB; Auto Scaling Group; RDS Multi-AZ standby; Route 53 health checks; ADL.
B: Junior Cloud Solutions Architect £28,000-£38,000; Associate Cloud Engineer £26,000-£35,000.
C: Design a HA web application on AWS. Opening: In Rimzenberg I designed CloudFront → Route 53 with health checks → Multi-AZ ALB → ASG across eu-west-2a and eu-west-2b → RDS MySQL Multi-AZ standby.
D: Most candidates name the services. Few explain why Multi-AZ standby not Read Replica, why ALB not NLB, or why CloudFront precedes Route 53.

business_impact.without_this: Rimzenberg enters another Black Friday with a single-AZ architecture. A repeat £180,000 outage is highly probable.
business_impact.with_this: A Multi-AZ HA architecture with CloudFront and Auto Scaling handles traffic spikes transparently.

## HA Web Application — Design Rationale

### Architecture Overview
[Users → CloudFront → Route 53 → ALB → EC2 ASG (Multi-AZ) → RDS Multi-AZ]

### Component List
| AWS Service | Role |
|---|---|
| Amazon CloudFront | |
| Amazon Route 53 | |
| Application Load Balancer | |
| Auto Scaling Group | |
| Amazon EC2 | |
| Amazon RDS MySQL (Multi-AZ) | |
| Amazon S3 | |

### How This Prevents Another Black Friday Outage
[Explain how each component addresses one of the three root causes]

### Architecture Decision Log

**Decision 1: CloudFront vs no CDN**
Chosen: | Alternative: | Rationale: []

**Decision 2: ALB vs NLB**
Chosen: | Alternative: | Rationale: []

**Decision 3: RDS Multi-AZ vs Read Replica**
Chosen: | Alternative: | Rationale: []

hints.text:
Flow: Users → CloudFront → Route 53 → ALB (multi-AZ) → EC2 ASG → RDS Multi-AZ.

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE:

User requests route through CloudFront (serving static assets from S3) to Route 53 with health checks, to Multi-AZ ALB, to ASG (min 2 EC2 t3.medium, eu-west-2a+b), to RDS MySQL Multi-AZ (auto-failover ~60s).

ADL: justify ALB vs NLB, Multi-AZ vs Read Replica, CloudFront vs direct Route 53.

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE:

ALB vs NLB: Chosen ALB. Rimzenberg uses HTTP/HTTPS and needs path-based routing. ALB operates at Layer 7. NLB operates at Layer 4 for TCP/UDP ultra-low latency — not required for a retail web application.

Complete ADL.

📋 EXPERT SAMPLE:

Multi-AZ vs Read Replica: Read Replicas require manual promotion (5-20 min). At midnight on Black Friday on-call may be unavailable. Multi-AZ auto-failover in 60-120 seconds reliably meets RTO ≤ 1 hour.

Auto Scaling vs fixed: Traffic shows extreme peaks (Black Friday 380%). Fixed capacity idle 90%+ of time. ASG scales 2→8+ instances during peaks.

phases.title: Phase 4 — Three-Tier Cloud Architecture
objectives: Design three-tier VPC with public, private, and database subnets
Create IAM least-privilege policies replacing AdministratorAccess roles
Fix the SG health check block and address the public-subnet RDS
Produce an ADL justifying design choices

content.text:
Five interview-style scenario questions based on the Rimzenberg simulation. Correct answers use the specific data, figures, and decisions from Phases 2-8.

Pass rate: 60% (3 of 5). Retry as many times as needed.

content.question: What was the actual root cause of the Black Friday outage? (Source: incident_log.csv INC-024)
options: A traffic spike overwhelmed single-AZ EC2 — installing Auto Scaling would have prevented the outage entirely
Three compounding failures: traffic spike (no ASG), RDS connection pool exhaustion at 100% (public subnet, 150 connection limit), and misconfigured Security Group blocking ALB health checks
The RDS was too small — upgrading to a larger instance class would have prevented the connection pool exhaustion
CloudFront was not configured — page loads became too slow causing users to time out
content.explanation: INC-024 shows three simultaneous failures: traffic_spike_pct=380 (no ASG), db_conn_pool_pct=100 (pool exhausted), sg_health_check_blocked=TRUE (ALB couldn't route to second instance). Auto Scaling alone would not have saved the day — if the SG health check block remained, the ALB couldn't route to new instances anyway.

content.question: Farah argued the RDS could stay in the public subnet with tighter security group rules. Marcus said it must move to a private subnet. Who was right?
options: Farah — tightening SG inbound rules to specific source IPs satisfies NCSC Principle 3
Marcus — NCSC Principle 3 requires network-level separation. A tighter SG on a public subnet still leaves the RDS with an internet route via the IGW
Both — either approach satisfies NCSC Principle 3 so the choice is based on cost
Neither — the real solution is migrating from RDS to DynamoDB
content.explanation: Marcus was right. NCSC Principle 3 requires separation at the network level — not just access control on a publicly routable resource. If the RDS is in a public subnet it has a route to the internet via the Internet Gateway regardless of how restrictive the SG is. Moving to a private subnet removes that internet route entirely. Farah's concern about reporting is valid but is addressed by AWS Systems Manager Session Manager.

content.question: What is the most complete justification for Lambda instead of scaling up EC2?
options: Lambda is always cheaper than EC2 for any workload

The order processing workload only spikes during checkout — always-on EC2 wastes approximately £4,100/month idle. Lambda pay-per-invocation eliminates idle cost, saving an estimated £29,520-£34,440/year at 60-70% reduction
Lambda is faster than EC2 because it has no cold start overhead
EC2 cannot handle the connection pool problem — Lambda automatically manages DynamoDB connections
content.explanation: Lambda is not always cheaper than EC2. The key is the usage pattern: Rimzenberg's order processing only generates traffic during checkout events. The cost_report.csv shows £4,100/month idle compute. At 60-70% saving the annual benefit is £29,520-£34,440. Lambda's other advantages (no connection pool, DynamoDB auto-scaling) are real but the primary justification is economic and usage-pattern specific.

content.question: Dev fixed the ALB health check security group issue manually twice but it kept reverting. What did his handover notes say was the likely cause?
options: A cron job was resetting the SG rule on a schedule
A drifted CloudFormation stack (rimzenberg-cfn-sg-01) was overwriting the manual fix each time — check the stack for drift and update the template rather than applying manual changes
The ALB itself was resetting the SG rule — a known AWS bug
Another engineer was reverting the change — check CloudTrail
content.explanation: Dev's handover notes (Stage 2) state: I suspect a CFN stack drift issue and mention CloudFormation stack rimzenberg-cfn-sg-01. This is marked CHECK THIS in the notes. Manual changes to CFN-managed resources are always overwritten by the next stack update or drift remediation — the fix must be applied by updating the CFN template.

content.question: Which combination of AWS services achieves RTO ≤ 1 hour and RPO ≤ 15 minutes?
options: S3 versioning (RPO) + EC2 AMI backups (RTO) — manual AMI restore can complete in under 1 hour
RDS Multi-AZ automatic failover (60-120 seconds for RTO) + RDS automated snapshots with 5-minute backup window (RPO) + Route 53 health checks for DNS failover + S3 CRR for static assets
RDS Read Replicas (RPO) + ALB health checks (RTO) — Read Replica can be promoted manually within 1 hour
AWS Backup daily snapshots (RPO ≤ 24 hours) + Multi-AZ EC2 (RTO ≤ 1 hour) — daily snapshots sufficient
content.explanation: RDS Multi-AZ automatic failover (60-120 seconds — well within 1-hour RTO). Automated snapshots with 5-minute backup window give data recovery within ≤ 15 minutes. Route 53 health checks enable automatic DNS failover. S3 CRR protects assets in a secondary region. Option A (AMI restore) is manual and unpredictable. Option C (Read Replica) requires manual promotion. Option D (daily snapshots) gives RPO of up to 24 hours — far worse than the 15-minute target.
